paper_title: Visible-light-harvesting reduction of CO2 to chemical fuels with pasmonic Ag@AgBr/CNT nanocomposites

catalyst.source: Visible-light-harvesting reduction of CO2 to chemical fuels with pasmonic Ag@AgBr/CNT nanocomposites
catalyst.context: Visible-light-harvesting reduction of CO2 to chemical fuels with pasmonic Ag@AgBr/CNT nanocomposites

catalyst.source: This paper presents the synthesis of visible-light-harvesting photocatalyst, AgAgBrcarbon nanotubes (CNT) nanocomposites, by the photoreduction of AgBrCNT, which was independently prepared by the deposition-precipitation method.
catalyst.context: This paper presents the synthesis of visible-light-harvesting photocatalyst, AgAgBrcarbon nanotubes (CNT) nanocomposites, by the photoreduction of AgBrCNT, which was independently prepared by the deposition-precipitation method.Nanocomposites with different carbon tubes lengths were characterized by X-ray diffraction, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques.AgAgBr nanopaparticles were found to be anchored onto the surface of CNT.EIS measurements suggested that the longer CNT in AgAgBrCNT were more efficient in transporting charges than that of the shorter length CNT, which agreed with the observed trend of photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 under visible light ( 420 nm) (AgAgBrCNT-L AgAgBrCNT-M AgAgBrCNT-S).The study on the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to methane, CO, methanol, and ethanol suggested that the reduction process favored under neutral and weak alkaline conditions.AgAgBrCNT can maintain the high stability in five repeated uses.

catalyst.source: The CNTs were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition from the CH 4 H 2 mixture at 700 C using Ni as a catalyst.
catalyst.context: Multi-walled carbon nanotubes were purchased from Shenzhen Nanotech.Port Co., Ltd. and were classified as CNT-S (CNT-1020 with 0.5 m length), CNT-M (CNT-1020 with 1-2 m length), and CNT-L (CNT-1020 with 5-15 m length).Details of CNTs are given in Table 1.The CNTs were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition from the CH 4 H 2 mixture at 700 C using Ni as a catalyst.The synthesized CNT were purified using the mixture of HNO 3 and H 2 SO 4 solutions, which decreased contents of metal catalyst and amorphous carbon.Carbon dioxide gas (99.99) was obtained from Foshan Ruike Gas Co. Ltd., China.Silver nitrate of analytical grade was purchased from Guangdong Guanhua Chemical Co. Ltd.China.Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, C 16 H 33 N (CH 3 ) 3 Br -; purity: 98) was obtained from Tianjing Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. in China.Other chemicals with analytical grade were obtained as reagents and were used without further purification.Deionized water was used throughout the study. The schematic diagram of synthesized AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites is presented in Fig. 2. AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites were prepared by the deposition-precipitation process in the presence of cation surfactant, CTAB, followed by photoreduction method according to the method reported in our previous work 22,23.An appropriate amount of CNT was oxidized by HNO 3 solution (HNO 3 :H 2 O 1:4, vv) for 1 h boiling, then washed by distilled water until the filtrate had neutral pH.The sample was added to 100 mL of CTAB aqueous solution with a concentration of 0.01 M (10 times above the CMC of CTAB 9.8 10 -4 M) 22, and the suspension was sonicated for 30 min, followed by magnetically stirring at room temperature for 12 h.Then 0.0012 mol of AgNO 3 in 2.3 mL of ammonia hydroxide (25 wt. NH 3 ) was rapidly added to the suspension.In this process, CTAB was adsorbed onto the surface of CNT at alkaline condition to limit the number of nucleation sites for the formation of AgBr aggregates; leading to well-dispersed AgBr anchored on CNT material.In addition, the amount of bromide ion from CTAB was more than sufficient to precipitate Ag from the added AgNO 3 in aqueous solution.The above solution was irradiated using a 150 W Xe arc lamp.The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with deionized water, dried at 75 C, and subsequently sintered in a muffle furnace at 400 C in the presence of N 2 for 3 h.In the control experiment, AgAgBr powders were prepared by a similar method without doping CNT. The prepared AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites powders were initially analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) method using a diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany) with radiation of Cu target (K , 1.54059A).Specific BET surface area was determined with an Autosorb-1-C ChemisorptionPhysisorption Analyzer (Quantachrome, USA) by nitrogen adsorption at 77.3 K. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with a JSM-6330F field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) using an acceleration voltage of 20 kV.The content of AgBr in the catalyst was determined by SEM-EDS-EBSD thermal field emission scanning electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan).AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites powders were then examined by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010HR).X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was recorded with the AlK line at 150 W using an X-ray Photoelectron SpectroscopyESCA (ESCALAB 250).Zeta potential measurements were performed using a ZetaPALS analyzer (Brookhaven, USA). The EIS measurements were carried out using a potentiostatgalvanostat (Autolab, PGSTAT302N) coupled to a lock-in amplifier (PGSTAT 302N).The impedance data were collected as a function of frequency scanned from the highest (1000 Hz) to the lowest (0.001 Hz) using the "single-sine" (lock-in amplifier) method.The measurements were conducted in a conventional Htype electrochemical Pyrex cell with a standard three-electrode assembly.This assembly had a AgAgBrCNT working electrode, a Pt wire as a counter electrode (area, 0.5 cm 1.0 cm), and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode.To prepare AgAgBrCNT electrodes, ITO plates were first cleaned by sonication in a mixture of acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 10 min.ITO plates were coated with AgAgBrCNT slurry, containing 3 wt.poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) as a binder.The loading amount of the electrode was controlled to be 1 mg cm -2 .Finally, AgAgBrCNT-coated ITO plates were dried at 70 C for 180 min to evaporate the excess NMP; yielding AgAgBrCNT electrodes.A bias was controlled to 300 mV applied to the photoelectrochemical system.The initial pH in the solution was adjusted to 5.8 using 0.1 M KCl and 0.1 M NaOH.A 71LX 150 W middle-pressure Xenon arc lamp having a filter to remove light 420 nm wavelength was used as the visible light source. Photocatalytic reduction was carried out in a stainless steel vessel with valves for evacuation and gas feeding reported in our previous work (Fig. S1), in which an O-ring sealed glass window was placed at the top for allowing light irradiation.A 150 W Xe lamp (Shanghai Aojia Lighting Appliance Co. Ltd.) with UV cutoff filter (providing visible light with 420 nm) was used for irradiation.The intensities of visible light were measured as 25 mW cm -2 and 22.4 mW cm -2 at the irradiated surface of the reactor and at the bottom of the solution, respectively.In a typical batch, 0.5 g of prepared AgAgBrCNT powders were suspended in 100 ml of 0.2 M KHCO 3 solution in the vessel.Prior to the irradiation, pure CO 2 (99.99) was passed through the solution for 30 min to remove the oxygen, and then a flow was closed to maintain a pressure of 7.5 MPa inside the reactor.During the reaction, powders were continuously agitated to prevent the sedimentation of the catalyst by a magnetic stirrer, and the photocatalytic reaction was carried out for 5 h at room temperature.After illumination, small aliquots of the suspension were withdrawn by syringe and filtered through Millipore membranes.The filtrates were subjected to analysis.Reaction products in liquid phase were analyzed using a gas chromatograph (Agilent HP6890N) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID) and a HP-5 capillary column (30 m 320 m 0.50 mm).Gas samples were collected with a gas-tight syringe through a septum and the products in gas phase were analyzed by GCMS Hewlett-Packard (HP) 6890 gas chromatography with a HP 5973 mass detector.A 60 m length 0.32 mm I.D 1.8 m film thickness HP-VOC column (Agilent Scientific, USA) was used.The oven was programmed as: the initial temperature of the column was 40 C hold for 2 min, followed by a ramp of 5 C min -1 to 120 C (1 min hold).

catalyst.source: In this study, AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with different tube lengths were successfully prepared.
catalyst.context: In this study, AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with different tube lengths were successfully prepared.The surface analysis of the nanocomposites showed the deposition of AgAgBr nanoparticles on CNT and this arrangement was able to promote the separation of charge in order to achieve efficient photoreduction of CO 2 under visible light irradiation.The charge separation increased with increase in length of CNT, which was confirmed by EIS measurements.Reduction of CO 2 resulted in methanol, CO, methanol, and ethanol and the yields of products depended on the length of CNT and the solution pH.The optimum pH range for efficient reduction of CO 2 was 7.0-8.5.Importantly, AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites were stable in the repeated uses for CO 2 reduction under visible light. Fig. 1.Schematic of photoinduced electron transfer in AgAgBrCNT under visible light. Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of synthesized AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites. Fig.3.XRD pattern of AgAgBrCNT-S (a), AgAgBrCNT-M (b), AgAgBrCNT-L (c) and AgAgBrCNT-L after repeated batch runs (d). Fig. 5. SEM images of AgAgBrCNT-S (a), AgAgBrCNT-M (b) and AgAgBrCNT-L (c), and EDS spectrum of AgAgBrCNT-L (d). Fig. 6.TEM image of AgAgBrCNT-L (a), and HRTEM images of AgAgBrCNT-L (b)-(c). Fig. 7. XPS peaks of (a) Ag 3d (b) Br 3d and (c) C 1s for AgAgBrCNT-L. Fig. 8. (a) Nyquist plots are shown here for AgAgBrCNT electrodes in dark and under visible light irradiation.Measurements were made at 300 mV in order to avoid the mass transport limited regime.The frequency range was 0.001-1000 Hz, with frequency decreasing from left to right along the x-axis, however very low frequency points over the used y-axis range are not shown here.(b) Shows the equivalent circuit used to model this system, including a component R1 series resistance, R2 the charge-transfer resistance, C double layer capacitance, and W Warburg impedance. Fig.9.Yield of photocatalytic products of AgAgBr and AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with different CNT length under visible-light irradiation for 5 h in pH 8.5 aqueous media. Fig. 10.Effect of pH value on photocatalytic products yield of AgAgBrCNT-L under 5 h visible light. Fig. 11.Photocatalytic products yield on number of batch runs for AgAgBrCNT-L. Properties of CNTs.Surface area (A surf ) was calculated from the adsorption-desorption isotherm of N2 at 77 K by the multipoint BET method.Ash contents were measured by heating the CNTs at 900 C for 10 h.). a Provided by the supplier.b Outer diameter (OD) measured by TEM, n 100.c The authors wish to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 20877025, 21273085), National Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. S2011010001836, S2011010003416), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 09lgpy20), Science and Technology Research Project from Guangzhou City (No. 12A42091603, 2010Z2-C1009) and Ministry of Science and Technology of China (No.

AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites were prepared by the deposition-precipitation process in the presence of cation surfactant, CTAB, followed by photoreduction method according to the method reported in our previous work 22,23.An appropriate amount of CNT was oxidized by HNO 3 solution (HNO 3 :H 2 O 1:4, vv) for 1 h boiling, then washed by distilled water until the filtrate had neutral pH.The sample was added to 100 mL of CTAB aqueous solution with a concentration of 0.01 M (10 times above the CMC of CTAB 9.8 10 -4 M) 22, and the suspension was sonicated for 30 min, followed by magnetically stirring at room temperature for 12 h.Then 0.0012 mol of AgNO 3 in 2.3 mL of ammonia hydroxide (25 wt. NH 3 ) was rapidly added to the suspension.In this process, CTAB was adsorbed onto the surface of CNT at alkaline condition to limit the number of nucleation sites for the formation of AgBr aggregates; leading to well-dispersed AgBr anchored on CNT material.In addition, the amount of bromide ion from CTAB was more than sufficient to precipitate Ag from the added AgNO 3 in aqueous solution.The above solution was irradiated using a 150 W Xe arc lamp.The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with deionized water, dried at 75 C, and subsequently sintered in a muffle furnace at 400 C in the presence of N 2 for 3 h.In the control experiment, AgAgBr powders were prepared by a similar method without doping CNT. The prepared AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites powders were initially analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) method using a diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany) with radiation of Cu target (K , 1.54059A).Specific BET surface area was determined with an Autosorb-1-C ChemisorptionPhysisorption Analyzer (Quantachrome, USA) by nitrogen adsorption at 77.3 K. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with a JSM-6330F field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) using an acceleration voltage of 20 kV.The content of AgBr in the catalyst was determined by SEM-EDS-EBSD thermal field emission scanning electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan).AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites powders were then examined by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010HR).X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was recorded with the AlK line at 150 W using an X-ray Photoelectron SpectroscopyESCA (ESCALAB 250).Zeta potential measurements were performed using a ZetaPALS analyzer (Brookhaven, USA). The EIS measurements were carried out using a potentiostatgalvanostat (Autolab, PGSTAT302N) coupled to a lock-in amplifier (PGSTAT 302N).The impedance data were collected as a function of frequency scanned from the highest (1000 Hz) to the lowest (0.001 Hz) using the "single-sine" (lock-in amplifier) method.The measurements were conducted in a conventional Htype electrochemical Pyrex cell with a standard three-electrode assembly.This assembly had a AgAgBrCNT working electrode, a Pt wire as a counter electrode (area, 0.5 cm 1.0 cm), and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode.To prepare AgAgBrCNT electrodes, ITO plates were first cleaned by sonication in a mixture of acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 10 min.ITO plates were coated with AgAgBrCNT slurry, containing 3 wt.poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) as a binder.The loading amount of the electrode was controlled to be 1 mg cm -2 .Finally, AgAgBrCNT-coated ITO plates were dried at 70 C for 180 min to evaporate the excess NMP; yielding AgAgBrCNT electrodes.A bias was controlled to 300 mV applied to the photoelectrochemical system.The initial pH in the solution was adjusted to 5.8 using 0.1 M KCl and 0.1 M NaOH.A 71LX 150 W middle-pressure Xenon arc lamp having a filter to remove light 420 nm wavelength was used as the visible light source. Photocatalytic reduction was carried out in a stainless steel vessel with valves for evacuation and gas feeding reported in our previous work (Fig. S1), in which an O-ring sealed glass window was placed at the top for allowing light irradiation.A 150 W Xe lamp (Shanghai Aojia Lighting Appliance Co. Ltd.) with UV cutoff filter (providing visible light with 420 nm) was used for irradiation.The intensities of visible light were measured as 25 mW cm -2 and 22.4 mW cm -2 at the irradiated surface of the reactor and at the bottom of the solution, respectively.In a typical batch, 0.5 g of prepared AgAgBrCNT powders were suspended in 100 ml of 0.2 M KHCO 3 solution in the vessel.Prior to the irradiation, pure CO 2 (99.99) was passed through the solution for 30 min to remove the oxygen, and then a flow was closed to maintain a pressure of 7.5 MPa inside the reactor.During the reaction, powders were continuously agitated to prevent the sedimentation of the catalyst by a magnetic stirrer, and the photocatalytic reaction was carried out for 5 h at room temperature.After illumination, small aliquots of the suspension were withdrawn by syringe and filtered through Millipore membranes.The filtrates were subjected to analysis.Reaction products in liquid phase were analyzed using a gas chromatograph (Agilent HP6890N) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID) and a HP-5 capillary column (30 m 320 m 0.50 mm).Gas samples were collected with a gas-tight syringe through a septum and the products in gas phase were analyzed by GCMS Hewlett-Packard (HP) 6890 gas chromatography with a HP 5973 mass detector.A 60 m length 0.32 mm I.D 1.8 m film thickness HP-VOC column (Agilent Scientific, USA) was used.The oven was programmed as: the initial temperature of the column was 40 C hold for 2 min, followed by a ramp of 5 C min -1 to 120 C (1 min hold).

reaction medium.source: The study on the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to methane, CO, methanol, and ethanol suggested that the reduction process favored under neutral and weak alkaline conditions.
reaction medium.context: This paper presents the synthesis of visible-light-harvesting photocatalyst, AgAgBrcarbon nanotubes (CNT) nanocomposites, by the photoreduction of AgBrCNT, which was independently prepared by the deposition-precipitation method.Nanocomposites with different carbon tubes lengths were characterized by X-ray diffraction, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques.AgAgBr nanopaparticles were found to be anchored onto the surface of CNT.EIS measurements suggested that the longer CNT in AgAgBrCNT were more efficient in transporting charges than that of the shorter length CNT, which agreed with the observed trend of photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 under visible light ( 420 nm) (AgAgBrCNT-L AgAgBrCNT-M AgAgBrCNT-S).The study on the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to methane, CO, methanol, and ethanol suggested that the reduction process favored under neutral and weak alkaline conditions.AgAgBrCNT can maintain the high stability in five repeated uses.

reaction medium.source: In a typical batch, 0.5 g of prepared AgAgBrCNT powders were suspended in 100 ml of 0.2 M KHCO 3 solution in the vessel.
reaction medium.context: Multi-walled carbon nanotubes were purchased from Shenzhen Nanotech.Port Co., Ltd. and were classified as CNT-S (CNT-1020 with 0.5 m length), CNT-M (CNT-1020 with 1-2 m length), and CNT-L (CNT-1020 with 5-15 m length).Details of CNTs are given in Table 1.The CNTs were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition from the CH 4 H 2 mixture at 700 C using Ni as a catalyst.The synthesized CNT were purified using the mixture of HNO 3 and H 2 SO 4 solutions, which decreased contents of metal catalyst and amorphous carbon.Carbon dioxide gas (99.99) was obtained from Foshan Ruike Gas Co. Ltd., China.Silver nitrate of analytical grade was purchased from Guangdong Guanhua Chemical Co. Ltd.China.Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, C 16 H 33 N (CH 3 ) 3 Br -; purity: 98) was obtained from Tianjing Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. in China.Other chemicals with analytical grade were obtained as reagents and were used without further purification.Deionized water was used throughout the study. The schematic diagram of synthesized AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites is presented in Fig. 2. NH 3 ) was rapidly added to the suspension.In this process, CTAB was adsorbed onto the surface of CNT at alkaline condition to limit the number of nucleation sites for the formation of AgBr aggregates; leading to well-dispersed AgBr anchored on CNT material.In addition, the amount of bromide ion from CTAB was more than sufficient to precipitate Ag from the added AgNO 3 in aqueous solution.The above solution was irradiated using a 150 W Xe arc lamp.The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with deionized water, dried at 75 C, and subsequently sintered in a muffle furnace at 400 C in the presence of N 2 for 3 h.In the control experiment, AgAgBr powders were prepared by a similar method without doping CNT. operation mode.source: Fig. 11.Photocatalytic products yield on number of batch runs for AgAgBrCNT-L.
operation mode.context: In this study, AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with different tube lengths were successfully prepared.The surface analysis of the nanocomposites showed the deposition of AgAgBr nanoparticles on CNT and this arrangement was able to promote the separation of charge in order to achieve efficient photoreduction of CO 2 under visible light irradiation.The charge separation increased with increase in length of CNT, which was confirmed by EIS measurements.Reduction of CO 2 resulted in methanol, CO, methanol, and ethanol and the yields of products depended on the length of CNT and the solution pH.The optimum pH range for efficient reduction of CO 2 was 7.0-8.5.Importantly, AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites were stable in the repeated uses for CO 2 reduction under visible light. Fig. 1.Schematic of photoinduced electron transfer in AgAgBrCNT under visible light. Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of synthesized AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites. Fig.3.XRD pattern of AgAgBrCNT-S (a), AgAgBrCNT-M (b), AgAgBrCNT-L (c) and AgAgBrCNT-L after repeated batch runs (d). Fig. 5. SEM images of AgAgBrCNT-S (a), AgAgBrCNT-M (b) and AgAgBrCNT-L (c), and EDS spectrum of AgAgBrCNT-L (d). Fig. 6.TEM image of AgAgBrCNT-L (a), and HRTEM images of AgAgBrCNT-L (b)-(c). Fig. 7. XPS peaks of (a) Ag 3d (b) Br 3d and (c) C 1s for AgAgBrCNT-L. Fig. 8. (a) Nyquist plots are shown here for AgAgBrCNT electrodes in dark and under visible light irradiation.Measurements were made at 300 mV in order to avoid the mass transport limited regime.The frequency range was 0.001-1000 Hz, with frequency decreasing from left to right along the x-axis, however very low frequency points over the used y-axis range are not shown here.(b) Shows the equivalent circuit used to model this system, including a component R1 series resistance, R2 the charge-transfer resistance, C double layer capacitance, and W Warburg impedance. Fig.9.Yield of photocatalytic products of AgAgBr and AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with different CNT length under visible-light irradiation for 5 h in pH 8.5 aqueous media. Fig. 10.Effect of pH value on photocatalytic products yield of AgAgBrCNT-L under 5 h visible light. Fig. 11.Photocatalytic products yield on number of batch runs for AgAgBrCNT-L. Properties of CNTs.Surface area (A surf ) was calculated from the adsorption-desorption isotherm of N2 at 77 K by the multipoint BET method.Ash contents were measured by heating the CNTs at 900 C for 10 h.). a Provided by the supplier.b Outer diameter (OD) measured by TEM, n 100.c The authors wish to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 20877025, 21273085), National Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. S2011010001836, S2011010003416), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 09lgpy20), Science and Technology Research Project from Guangzhou City (No. 12A42091603, 2010Z2-C1009) and Ministry of Science and Technology of China (No.
